mcqs.question: Consider a 3-tape Turing Machine that recognizes language L in time O(n). What is the worst-case time complexity when this TM is simulated on a single-tape TM?
mcqs.explanation: A k-tape TM running in time T(n) can be simulated by a single-tape TM in O(T(n)²) time. Since the original runs in O(n), the simulation runs in O(n²). This quadratic overhead is the price for reducing multiple tapes to one.

mcqs.question: In the simulation of a Multi-tape TM by a single-tape TM, what is the purpose of the separator symbols (like #)?

options.text: They mark the beginning and end of the tape for acceptance/rejection
They separate the k virtual tapes on the single physical tape
They represent blank symbols in the original TM's alphabet
They indicate the current state of the simulation

mcqs.explanation: The simulation encodes k virtual tapes on a single tape by using separator symbols (#) to delimit each virtual tape. This allows the single-tape TM to maintain the content and head positions of all k tapes simultaneously.

mcqs.question: Let M be a Non-deterministic Turing Machine with branching factor at most 3 that accepts language L in time O(n²). What is the time complexity of the equivalent deterministic TM (using BFS simulation)?
mcqs.explanation: The DTM simulates the NDTM by exploring its configuration tree. With branching factor b=3 and original running time T(n)=n², the DTM explores up to b^T(n) = 3^(n²) configurations in the worst case, giving exponential time complexity.

mcqs.question: Which of the following statements about Turing Machine extensions is FALSE?

options.text: Multi-tape TMs are more convenient but computationally equivalent to single-tape TMs
Non-deterministic TMs and deterministic TMs accept the same languages ( recursively enumerable languages)
A Universal Turing Machine can simulate any other TM but requires infinite tape for all simulations
Linear Bounded Automata accept exactly the context-sensitive languages

mcqs.explanation: A UTM can simulate any other TM on any input using only the space needed for that particular simulation. It doesn't require infinite tape - it uses exactly the tape the simulated TM would use, encoded appropriately. This is false.

mcqs.question: In the formal definition of a Non-deterministic Turing Machine, the transition function δ maps to:
mcqs.explanation: In an NDTM, δ: Q × Γ → P(Q × Γ × {L,R}) maps each (state, tape symbol) pair to a SET of possible moves (not a single value). The power set P allows multiple next configurations, creating branching computation paths.

mcqs.question: The Universal Turing Machine demonstrates which fundamental concept of computation?

options.text: The equivalence of regular and context-free languages

mcqs.explanation: The UTM stores both the program (encoding of the TM to simulate) and data (input string) on its tape - the same medium. This is exactly the stored-program concept underlying all modern computers, where instructions are data.

mcqs.question: A Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA) is restricted in that:

options.text: It can only use a fixed, predetermined amount of tape
Its tape usage is bounded by a linear function of the input length
It cannot have more than one tape

mcqs.explanation: An LBA is a restricted TM where the tape head cannot move beyond the boundaries containing the input (the portion of tape initially marked). Thus, tape usage is bounded by a linear function of input length (specifically, exactly the input space).

mcqs.question: If a language L is accepted by a Non-deterministic Turing Machine in polynomial time, what can we say about its deterministic time complexity?

options.text: L must also be in P (deterministic polynomial time)
L is definitely not in P unless P=NP
L is in EXPTIME (deterministic exponential time)

mcqs.explanation: By the deterministic simulation of NDTM, any language accepted by an NDTM in time T(n) is accepted by a DTM in time O(b^T(n)) for branching factor b. Polynomial-time NDTM languages (NP) are in EXPTIME (deterministic exponential time). This is the basis for the P vs NP question.

mcqs.question: When simulating a 2-tape TM on a single-tape TM, what is the maximum number of passes required to simulate ONE step of the original machine?

options.text: n passes where n is input length

mcqs.explanation: To simulate one step, the single-tape TM must first scan right to find all current head positions on both virtual tapes, then scan left to update symbols and move heads accordingly. This requires 2 passes (right-to-end, then left-to-start).

mcqs.question: Which class of languages is accepted by Linear Bounded Automata?
mcqs.explanation: LBAs accept exactly the context-sensitive languages, which are the Type-1 languages in the Chomsky hierarchy. These are languages generated by context-sensitive grammars, characterized by non-contracting production rules.

mcqs.question: The Church-Turing Thesis states that:

options.text: All computable functions can be computed by some Turing Machine
Every DTM can be simulated by an NDTM

mcqs.explanation: The Church-Turing Thesis is the hypothesis that any function that is 'computable' (in the intuitive sense) can be computed by a Turing Machine. This unifies the notion of computability across different computational models.

mcqs.question: In a breadth-first search simulation of an NDTM by a DTM, configurations are typically stored on the tape using:

options.text: A priority queue sorted by creation time
Multiple delimiter-separated sections with markers for current exploration depth

mcqs.explanation: The DTM must keep track of unexplored configurations at each depth level. This is typically done using delimiters (like $ and #) to separate different configuration levels, allowing systematic exploration from shallower to deeper branches to find an accepting path.

mcqs.question: What is the key difference between a basic TM and a Universal TM in terms of input?

options.text: UTM takes two inputs: encoding of a TM and encoding of an input string
UTM has no input alphabet restriction
UTM can only simulate TMs with smaller state sets

mcqs.explanation: A UTM is a universal simulator - it takes as input a description/encoding of any other Turing Machine M (in some standard encoding like Gödel numbering) plus an encoding of the input string w. It then simulates M running on w.

mcqs.question: If M is a k-tape TM with transition function δ, what additional information must the simulating single-tape TM maintain?

options.text: Only the current states of M
Only the symbols under each virtual head
Both current states and positions of all k heads, encoded on the single tape
A copy of the original transition function

mcqs.explanation: The single-tape simulation must track: (1) The current state of M, (2) The current symbol under each of the k heads, (3) The positions of each of the k heads. All this information is encoded on the single tape using special markers and separators.

mcqs.question: Which of the following Turing Machine extensions increases computational power (accepts more languages)?

options.text: None - all are computationally equivalent to basic TM

mcqs.explanation: According to the Church-Turing Thesis and proven simulation results, all these extensions are computationally equivalent to the basic single-tape deterministic TM. They can all simulate each other (with potentially different time complexities). They accept exactly the recursively enumerable languages.
